content.problemStatement:
Chef is given a tree with NNN nodes and a positive integer KKK. Each node is assigned a value AiA_iAiвЂ‹.

Chef has to divide the tree into a forest of exactly KKK trees, numbered from 111 to KKK, such that each node belongs to **exactly one** tree.

Let BiB_iBiвЂ‹ denote the bitwise XOR of the values of all nodes belonging to the ithi^{th}ith tree.

Find the **maximum** possible value of B1B_1B1вЂ‹ &\&& B2B_2B2вЂ‹ &вЂ¦\& \dots&вЂ¦ &\&& BKB_KBKвЂ‹, where &\&& denotes the bitwise AND operation.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines:

The first line contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK вЂ” the number of nodes in the tree and a positive integer as mentioned in the statement, respectively.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,вЂ¦,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1вЂ‹,A2вЂ‹,вЂ¦,ANвЂ‹, the values assigned to each node.
The next (Nв€’1)(N-1)(Nв€’1) lines describe the edges. The ithi^{th}ith of these (Nв€’1)(N-1)(Nв€’1) lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iuiвЂ‹ and viv_iviвЂ‹, denoting an edge between nodes uiu_iuiвЂ‹ and viv_iviвЂ‹.




Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer, the **maximum** possible value of B1B_1B1вЂ‹ &\&& B2B_2B2вЂ‹ &вЂ¦\& \dots&вЂ¦ &\&& BKB_KBKвЂ‹
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2в‹…1052 \cdot 10^52в‹…105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: A possible way of dividing the nodes into 222 trees is [1,3,6][1,3,6][1,3,6] and [2,4,5][2,4,5][2,4,5].

B1=A1вЉ•A3вЉ•A6=1вЉ•2вЉ•5=6B_1 = A_1\oplus A_3\oplus A_6 = 1\oplus 2\oplus 5 = 6B1вЂ‹=A1вЂ‹вЉ•A3вЂ‹вЉ•A6вЂ‹=1вЉ•2вЉ•5=6
B2=A2вЉ•A4вЉ•A5=4вЉ•2вЉ•1=7B_2 = A_2\oplus A_4\oplus A_5 = 4\oplus 2\oplus 1 = 7B2вЂ‹=A2вЂ‹вЉ•A4вЂ‹вЉ•A5вЂ‹=4вЉ•2вЉ•1=7

Thus, B1B_1B1вЂ‹ &\&& B2=6B_2 = 6B2вЂ‹=6 &\&& 7=67 = 67=6.
Note that this is the maximum possible value of B1B_1B1вЂ‹ &\&& B2B_2B2вЂ‹ you can obtain.
Another way of dividing is [1,2,4,5][1,2,4,5][1,2,4,5] and [3,6][3,6][3,6], which leads to the same answer.
Test case 222: A possible way of dividing the nodes into 333 trees is [1,3,6,7,8][1,3,6,7,8][1,3,6,7,8], [4][4][4], and [2,5][2,5][2,5]

B1=A1вЉ•A3вЉ•A6вЉ•A7вЉ•A8=1вЉ•5вЉ•9вЉ•0вЉ•8=5B_1 = A_1\oplus A_3\oplus A_6\oplus A_7\oplus A_8 = 1\oplus 5\oplus 9 \oplus 0\oplus 8 = 5B1вЂ‹=A1вЂ‹вЉ•A3вЂ‹вЉ•A6вЂ‹вЉ•A7вЂ‹вЉ•A8вЂ‹=1вЉ•5вЉ•9вЉ•0вЉ•8=5
B2=A4=14B_2 = A_4 = 14B2вЂ‹=A4вЂ‹=14
B3=A2вЉ•A5=13вЉ•2=15B_3 = A_2\oplus A_5 = 13\oplus 2 = 15B3вЂ‹=A2вЂ‹вЉ•A5вЂ‹=13вЉ•2=15.

Thus, B1B_1B1вЂ‹ &\&& B2B_2B2вЂ‹ &\&& B3=5B_3 = 5B3вЂ‹=5 &\&& 141414 &\&& &\&& 15=415 = 415=4.
Note that this is the maximum possible value of B1B_1B1вЂ‹ &\&& B2B_2B2вЂ‹ &\&& B3B_3B3вЂ‹ you can obtain.
